tax: Classified as Aberration rather than Monstrosity because the cognitive capacity their mimicry requires cannot be accounted for by their recoverable neurology; mechanism of accurate mimicry selection is unknown and has not yielded to dissection.
ap: Variable by target: appear as a Glowcap colony, a Phantomyces web, or a Photophora signaling organism. The tell is that nothing moves toward them to graze.
eco: Lure-predators survive by hijacking the assumptions of cave animals and travelers. Their bodies flatten into biofilm, mineral varnish, corpse-sheen, or light-producing patches until prey approaches within reach. They feed on fluids, nerves, and attention, leaving victims positioned as new bait rather than as scattered remains.
beh: A lure-predator is patient in the way a trap is patient. It does not chase unless the ambush has already failed; instead it tests sounds, light pulses, scent traces, and familiar silhouettes until the prey completes the approach voluntarily. Repeated encounters suggest local learning rather than instinct alone.
traitsText: Lure-Predators traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: Gorge-present. Trait tags: bioluminescence.
traits: The defining traits are a concealed main body, a specialized attractive surface, sudden radial mouth deployment, and sensory organs distributed through the lure rather than concentrated in a head. In torchlight the lure often looks more real than its surroundings, because it is actively exaggerating the cue it imitates.
abilities: Their abilities include bioluminescent mimicry, tactile adhesive fields, reflexive mouth-opening along hidden seams, and limited empathic or sensory baiting. Stronger specimens can imitate the rhythm of a wounded animal, a lantern behind fog, or the reflected eye-shine of a companion.
bg: In the Throat they are route hazards rather than roaming predators. A corridor with too many perfect glow-patches, too-clean bones, or bodies seated upright near the wall is treated as contaminated until scraped, burned, and watched through an entire dark cycle.
g: Bioluminescent ambush predators that mimic the light-signatures of non-predatory organisms — a behavior requiring the ability to model the perceptual world of prey species with a nervous system that is not large enough to do this.
